prompt: Explain what "meaning" means. Not "the meaning of X" — the concept itself.
response:
“Meaning” is the invisible thread that turns a mere event, mark, or sound into something *about* something else.  
It is the asymmetry between a physical thing and what that thing *points to*, *evokes*, or *counts as* for an interpreter.  

A cloud is only condensed water; but when it is *taken* as a sign of rain tomorrow, the cloud carries meaning.  
The ink-shape “dog” is only pigment on paper; yet for anyone who knows English it *stands in for* every hairy, barking mammal it is not.  
A kiss on the forehead is only contact between lips and skin; yet in the right context it *counts as* forgiveness, blessing, or farewell.

Meaning, then, is not an extra property like mass or color; it is a *relation*—a three-place relation between  
(1) a vehicle (the mark, gesture, word, neuron-firing),  
(2) what the vehicle maps onto, indicates, or updates (the “content”), and  
(3) the interpreter whose dispositions, history, or norms make the mapping possible.

Without that interpreter-relative asymmetry—vehicle ≠ content—nothing is *about* anything; the world just clacks along in causal chains. Meaning is the subtle tilt that lets some of those chains become *messages*, *thoughts*, *promises*, *lies*, *equations*, *jokes*.
